jokes.text: Why don't we top it off by reading to old people?
jokes.setup: Marge asks if anything is more fun than a Sunday drive
jokes.punchline: Lisa sarcastically suggests the even more boring activity of reading to old people
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment - her precocious sarcasm and intellectual superiority complex. Well-structured setup/punchline with economy of language.

jokes.text: Well, I think in these days of petro-terrorists and ozone depletion, a Sunday drive reeks of bio-hubris.
jokes.setup: Lisa continuing to critique the Sunday drive concept
jokes.punchline: Over-the-top environmental buzzword soup delivered with complete seriousness
jokes.explanation: Classic Lisa - using unnecessarily complex environmental terminology. 'Bio-hubris' is a perfect made-up term that sounds plausible but is absurd.

jokes.text: Can't I get a cup of coffee without doing a monkey dance for you freaks? The fishing hat means leave me alone!
jokes.setup: Family encounters Krusty the Clown in public
jokes.punchline: Krusty's hostile reaction and attempt to use his fishing hat as an invisibility cloak
jokes.explanation: Great character moment for Krusty - the idea that a fishing hat serves as some kind of 'do not disturb' sign is perfectly absurd and in character.

jokes.text: Always nice to see him.
jokes.setup: Krusty just screamed at them and called them freaks
jokes.punchline: Marge's polite, oblivious response
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge response - maintaining politeness in the face of obvious rudeness. Economy and timing are excellent.

jokes.text: I cleaned out the garage and lost 50 pounds.
jokes.setup: Kids wonder how Homer got out of the Sunday drive
jokes.punchline: Visual reveal of Homer looking identical, having apparently thrown away 50 pounds of possessions rather than lost weight
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection - audience expects weight loss, gets literal interpretation. Visual component likely shows him looking exactly the same.

jokes.text: It seems a shame to chuck out these cross-country skis.
jokes.setup: Homer is cleaning out garage, finds cross-country skis
jokes.punchline: Sentiment about throwing away skis in Springfield (which has no snow/mountains)
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about keeping inappropriate items. Sets up geographic absurdity but doesn't fully capitalize on it.

jokes.text: Stupid spider! Afraid of dying?
jokes.setup: Spider appears immediately after Homer said they don't have spiders
jokes.punchline: Homer taunts the spider about being afraid of dying
jokes.explanation: Homer's psychological warfare against a spider is absurd and in character. The taunt itself is funny in its specificity.

jokes.text: Spider poison is people poison?!
jokes.setup: Homer presumably reading warning label on spider killer
jokes.punchline: Shocked realization that poison meant to kill spiders might also kill people
jokes.explanation: Peak Homer stupidity - the genuine surprise that poison designed to kill living things might kill him. The phrasing is perfect.

jokes.text: I learned CPR while waiting for other kids to finish their math tests.
jokes.setup: Lisa arrives to help with Homer's spider poisoning
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation that she's so academically advanced she had time to learn CPR during math class
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character detail - her academic superiority expressed through this casual aside about having extra time to learn life-saving skills.

jokes.text: Who put those spiders in my mouth?!
jokes.setup: Homer wakes up from spider poisoning incident
jokes.punchline: First concern is about spiders somehow ending up in his mouth
jokes.explanation: Absurd post-unconsciousness complaint. The specific imagery of spiders in mouth and the accusatory tone work well.

jokes.text: Don't worry, if I croak, you'll marry Lenny... or Moe. The winner will be determined by a card game I invented.
jokes.setup: Marge worries about what would happen if Homer died
jokes.punchline: Homer has planned her remarriage to his bar buddies via custom card game
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - the elaborate planning for something inappropriate, and the card game detail elevates it. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Have you ever had a heart attack? Haven't we all?
jokes.setup: Insurance agent asks about Homer's medical history
jokes.punchline: Homer treats heart attacks as universal human experience
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and delivery. Homer's casual attitude toward serious medical events, delivered with philosophical resignation.

jokes.text: Strokes? None. No, wait, three. Since the last one, I don't remember so good.
jokes.setup: Continuing medical history questions
jokes.punchline: Homer corrects himself about strokes, then explains memory problems from most recent stroke
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about brain damage affecting memory, creating self-referential loop. The grammar ('remember so good') sells the damage.

jokes.text: Shh! I want her to think I'm cool!
jokes.setup: Marge corrects Homer about smoking when he tells insurance agent he smokes
jokes.punchline: Homer wants to impress insurance agent by pretending to smoke
jokes.explanation: Homer's misguided attempt to seem impressive to someone evaluating his health risks. The adolescent desire to seem 'cool' is perfect.

jokes.text: My hemophilia!
jokes.setup: Homer apparently signing insurance forms
jokes.punchline: Paper cut triggers dramatic revelation of serious bleeding disorder
jokes.explanation: Escalation of Homer's medical problems revealed through minor injury. The dramatic delivery sells the absurdity.

jokes.text: Don't thank me. Thank our many debts and obligations.
jokes.setup: TV movie wife thanks husband for their lifestyle
jokes.punchline: Husband sarcastically credits their financial problems
jokes.explanation: Parody of TV movie dialogue - the ironic twist on gratitude. Works as satire of melodramatic television.

jokes.text: You should have thought of that before you had children with a dead man.
jokes.setup: TV movie widow asks how she'll feed her family
jokes.punchline: Doctor blames her for not anticipating her husband's death
jokes.explanation: Absurdly callous medical advice. The logical impossibility of planning for unpredictable death makes it funny rather than just cruel.

jokes.text: The real Brenda later went to Harvard Medical School.. As a cadaver.
jokes.setup: TV movie narrator seems to provide uplifting epilogue about Brenda
jokes.punchline: Reveals she went to medical school as a dead body for dissection
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - sets up inspirational ending then delivers dark punchline. 'As a cadaver' is perfectly timed revelation.

jokes.text: You just have to assemble it yourself.
jokes.setup: Marge explains Budget-O's cereal is cheaper than name brand
jokes.punchline: The cereal requires assembly (likely showing pieces of cereal that need to be put together)
jokes.explanation: Absurd take on generic products - the idea of DIY cereal. Visual component likely shows comically complex assembly required.

jokes.text: You mean when the big tuna goes belly-up?
jokes.setup: Marge mentions saving for a rainy day
jokes.punchline: Bart interprets 'rainy day' as 'when the big tuna goes belly-up' (death metaphor)
jokes.explanation: Creative euphemism for death that sounds like something Bart would say. 'Big tuna' as slang for father figure is inventive.

jokes.text: I paid a bundle for overnight delivery.
jokes.setup: Homer ordered money-saving tapes
jokes.punchline: Spent extra money for rush delivery of money-saving advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer contradiction - defeats the purpose of saving money by spending money to learn about saving money faster.

jokes.text: Okay, I'll just hire a haulage firm to cart them away... at my expense!
jokes.setup: Marge mentions library book has same money-saving information
jokes.punchline: Homer's solution to dispose of the now-useless tapes involves more spending
jokes.explanation: Homer doubles down on wasteful spending to solve his wasteful spending problem. The formal language ('haulage firm') adds to the absurdity.

jokes.text: No, a whole team of lawyers, who will dine on mustard flown in from the Orient.
jokes.setup: Homer threatening to get a lawyer about directory assistance charges
jokes.punchline: Escalates to team of lawyers with expensive imported condiment requirements
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - the specificity of 'mustard flown in from the Orient' is perfectly absurd. Great example of Homer's rage-spiral logic.

jokes.text: People died in those shirts.
jokes.setup: Lisa complaining about wearing Goodwill clothes
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact observation about the morbid history of secondhand clothing
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark humor delivered with Lisa's matter-of-fact intelligence. The brevity and directness make it hit harder.

jokes.text: Drinking beer out of a thermos like an animal.
jokes.setup: Homer dealing with beer budget restrictions
jokes.punchline: Sees drinking from thermos as degrading rather than the beer itself being the issue
jokes.explanation: Homer's priorities - the vessel matters more than the venue or situation. Good character moment about his drinking standards.

jokes.text: Last year, you spent $5,000 on donuts, $2,000 on scalp massages, $500 on body glitter...
jokes.setup: Marge listing Homer's frivolous expenses
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd and personal grooming expenses with specific dollar amounts
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from reasonable (donuts) to absurd (body glitter). The specific amounts make Homer's vanity spending more ridiculous.

jokes.text: You waltz in there at 10:30, take a nap on the toilet, then sit around Googling your own name until lunch.
jokes.setup: Homer claims to work hard while Marge lounges around
jokes.punchline: Marge's detailed description of Homer's actual 'work' day
jokes.explanation: Perfect character assassination - the specificity makes it believable and funnier. 'Googling your own name' is a perfect modern touch.

jokes.text: You shouted it while we were making love.
jokes.setup: Homer asks who told Marge about his work habits
jokes.punchline: Homer apparently bragged about slacking off during intimate moments
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cringe moment - Homer's complete lack of situational awareness. The idea of work-bragging during sex is perfectly inappropriate.

jokes.text: I invented some kind of stove.
jokes.setup: Franklin correcting Homer about never being president
jokes.punchline: Downplays his major invention of the Franklin stove
jokes.explanation: Nice understatement from Franklin about his historical significance. The vague 'some kind of stove' sells the false modesty.

jokes.text: Well, I invented a Popsicle made of Mountain Dew.
jokes.setup: Franklin mentions inventing a stove
jokes.punchline: Homer compares his junk food creation to Franklin's major invention
jokes.explanation: Homer's misguided attempt to relate to a founding father. The specific combination of Mountain Dew and Popsicle is perfectly Homer.

jokes.text: I spent our savings on the down payment for a motor home.
jokes.setup: Marge asks if Homer spent savings on motor home
jokes.punchline: Reveals he only spent savings on down payment, implying ongoing debt
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - just when you think Homer's financial irresponsibility is revealed, it gets worse. The correction makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I need a castle where I can be king!
jokes.setup: Homer defending his motor home purchase
jokes.punchline: Frames RV ownership in grandiose royal terms
jokes.explanation: Homer's need for dominance expressed through medieval metaphor. The king/castle comparison elevates a motor home to absurd status.

jokes.text: That's my half-assed dad.
jokes.setup: Homer is leaving the family but staying on their property
jokes.punchline: Lisa's clinical assessment of Homer's commitment level
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa observation - clinical and accurate. 'Half-assed' perfectly captures Homer's approach to rebellion.

jokes.text: You're a bread compartment!
jokes.setup: Homer tells Bart he's sitting in a bread compartment
jokes.punchline: Bart uses 'bread compartment' as a comeback insult
jokes.explanation: Classic kid comeback - turning a mundane object into an insult. Perfect for Bart's character and the RV setting.

jokes.text: You can wear swimsuits instead of underwear!
jokes.setup: Homer competing with Marge for kids' attention
jokes.punchline: Offers increasingly inappropriate parental permissiveness
jokes.explanation: Homer's desperation leads to absurd parental bribes. The specificity of swimsuits as underwear shows his lack of boundaries.

jokes.text: We use jokes to mask our pain at the death of our son.
jokes.setup: Continuing conversation with RV couple
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation of tragic backstory as explanation for humor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke about comedy itself. The matter-of-fact delivery of devastating information is perfectly dark.

jokes.text: I have a backyard that makes my front yard look like an idiot.
jokes.setup: RV owner looking for place to park
jokes.punchline: Homer brags about yard size using personification of landscape
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist metaphor - treating yards as if they can be embarrassed. The unusual comparison structure is very quotable.

jokes.text: If my wife asks, you're here for a Boz Skaggs concert.
jokes.setup: Homer welcoming RV convoy to his yard
jokes.punchline: Pre-planned lie involving obscure 70s musician
jokes.explanation: Good character detail - Homer's cover story is oddly specific and dated. Boz Scaggs is perfect choice for implausible concert.

jokes.text: This is a house!
jokes.setup: RV owner compliments what he thinks is Marge's RV
jokes.punchline: Marge's mortified realization that their house is being mistaken for a motor home
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment - Marge's horror at her home being mistaken for an RV. The economy and timing are excellent.

jokes.text: I know another place you can go! To sleep.
jokes.setup: RV singers mention all the places they've been
jokes.punchline: Marge redirects their travels to bedtime
jokes.explanation: Classic parent shutdown of annoying activity. Good structure with the misdirection, though not particularly original.

jokes.text: It's not golden brown, it's not brown, it's not nothin'.
jokes.setup: RV owner complaining about power outage ruining his sandwich
jokes.punchline: Increasingly frustrated description of sandwich's incomplete cooking state
jokes.explanation: Good escalation in food complaint - from specific color expectation to complete negation. The grammar adds to the character.

jokes.text: That's no marriage bed. It's a loveless slab of bossiness!
jokes.setup: RV owner suggests Homer belongs in bed with wife
jokes.punchline: Homer's poetic description of his marital bed
jokes.explanation: Great metaphor for marital problems. 'Loveless slab of bossiness' is both poetic and pathetic, perfect for Homer's self-pity.

jokes.text: You hide food in my hair.
jokes.setup: Homer says Marge isn't perfect either
jokes.punchline: Marge's specific complaint about Homer's food storage habits
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific and absurd marital complaint. The image of Homer using Marge's hair as food storage is perfectly grotesque.

jokes.text: No. I don't see Dad doing all that paperwork.
jokes.setup: Bart asks if parents will get divorced
jokes.punchline: Lisa's practical assessment of Homer's administrative capabilities
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa analysis - reducing marital crisis to administrative incompetence. Shows her clinical understanding of her father.

jokes.text: That was my Woodstock!
jokes.setup: Homer defending his onion ring story
jokes.punchline: Compares fast food mix-up to legendary music festival
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Homer's tendency to inflate mundane experiences. Comparing onion ring to Woodstock is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Duh! It's Kamper Kong.
jokes.setup: Bart asks how Lisa knows which blimp belongs to RV dealer
jokes.punchline: Visual pun on King Kong with camping/RV theme
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective pun on King Kong. The visual element of giant gorilla blimp selling RVs works well for Simpsons style.

jokes.text: Tie me to the Kong and cut its tethers.
jokes.setup: RV dealer learns kids are trying to return vehicle
jokes.punchline: Dramatic plan to ride the blimp to stop the return
jokes.explanation: Absurd action movie solution to business problem. The King Kong reference and dramatic escalation work for cartoon logic.

jokes.text: It was more than a motor home. It was a car I could go to the bathroom in.
jokes.setup: Homer lamenting loss of his RV
jokes.punchline: Reduces complex vehicle to its bathroom functionality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - the profound reduced to the practical. The poetic structure followed by crude reality is excellent.

jokes.text: This is a parent's worst nightmare! They've stolen a car and they're home alone.
jokes.setup: Kids have taken RV to return it
jokes.punchline: Marge lists two movie scenarios as real parental fears
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture reference combination. Marge's tendency to reference movies as real life scenarios fits her character.

jokes.text: Dino-- short for dinosaur? Remember to ask Jeeves.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to read his own handwriting on map
jokes.punchline: Notes to himself show his thought process and outdated internet habits
jokes.explanation: Good character detail about Homer's note-taking and outdated tech references. Ask Jeeves reference dates the episode nicely.

jokes.text: When I was a kid, my mom would give me an empty egg carton and I'd pretend it was a spaceship to the moon. You'll never guess what I used for astronauts.
jokes.setup: Driver commenting on kids driving RVs vs. simpler childhood
jokes.punchline: Sets up obvious 'eggs as astronauts' conclusion
jokes.explanation: Predictable setup with obvious punchline. The nostalgia angle works but the joke is telegraphed too clearly.

jokes.text: French it up, lover boy!
jokes.setup: Bart demanding parents kiss to prove they've made up
jokes.punchline: Bart's inappropriate demand for passionate kissing style
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - kid making inappropriate demands about parental intimacy. Bart's casual crudeness is in character.

jokes.text: And we must get these Season One Northern Exposure DVDs to Kahramanmaras.
jokes.setup: Captain explaining why he can't help rescue the kids
jokes.punchline: Urgent delivery of obscure TV show DVDs to random Turkish city
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific absurdist cargo. Northern Exposure DVDs to obscure Turkish city is perfectly random international commerce.

jokes.text: Bring back our children, you Cyprus-splitting jerks!
jokes.setup: Homer angry at Turkish ship taking his kids
jokes.punchline: Insults Turks with specific historical/political grievance
jokes.explanation: Homer's surprising knowledge of Cyprus conflict for insult purposes. Shows his ability to pull random historical facts when angry.

jokes.text: It is a shame you dress like a Lebanese prostitute.
jokes.setup: Captain praising Marge's wisdom
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment about her appearance
jokes.explanation: Cultural insensitivity played for comedy. The contrast between compliment and insult works structurally but relies on stereotype.

jokes.text: Water damage-- can't be returned. I win again!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to return wet RV to dealer
jokes.punchline: Homer treats obvious failure as victory
jokes.explanation: Homer's ability to reframe complete failure as success. His delusional optimism in face of obvious defeat is classic.

jokes.text: Dude, she seemed tense, so we put a little hashish in her meal.
jokes.setup: Marge taking the RV situation surprisingly well
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of drugging her food
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about casual drug administration. The matter-of-fact delivery and 'dude' casualness make it work.

jokes.text: Which came first, turkey the bird or Turkey the you guys?
jokes.setup: Marge under influence of hashish
jokes.punchline: Pseudo-philosophical question about etymology
jokes.explanation: Good stoned humor - the kind of question that seems profound while under influence. Marge's altered state is well-characterized.
